title_en: Kitab al-Taharah: Purity and Its Categories
content_en:
The chapter on purity (taharah) in al-Umm lays out al-Shafi'i's systematic approach to one of the most foundational topics in Islamic law. Purity is a precondition for prayer, Quran recitation in the physical sense, and the circumambulation of the Ka'bah, making its mastery essential for every Muslim. Al-Shafi'i treats the topic with characteristic precision, classifying purity into ritual purity (taharah hadathiyyah) — the removal of states of minor impurity (hadath asghar) and major impurity (hadath akbar) — and physical purity (taharah khabathiyyah), which involves the removal of filth (najasah) from the body, clothing, and place of prayer.

Water is the primary agent of purification. Al-Shafi'i divides water into pure and purifying (tahur), pure but not purifying (tahir), and impure (najis). Water that has been used for obligatory ritual washing is pure itself but, in the Shafi'i school, no longer capable of purifying others — a position that distinguishes it from the Hanafi school, which permits used water to purify. Large bodies of water that exceed two qullahs (roughly 190 liters) are not rendered impure by the mere contact of impurity unless their color, smell, or taste changes. This quantitative threshold comes from a famous hadith narrated by Ibn Umar, which al-Shafi'i treats as authoritative.

For wudu, al-Shafi'i specifies the six obligatory acts: intention (niyyah), washing the face, washing the hands to the elbows, wiping a part of the head, washing the feet to the ankles, and performing these in the prescribed sequence (tartib). Sequence is obligatory in the Shafi'i school, a point contested by the Hanafi school, which considers it only recommended. Continuity (muwalat), or performing the acts without undue interruption, is recommended but not obligatory in the dominant Shafi'i position.

The invalidators of wudu in the Shafi'i school include anything exiting from the private parts, deep sleep that removes consciousness, touching the genitalia with the bare palm, and — in one of the most well-known Shafi'i positions — skin contact between a marriageable man and woman (al-lams). This last rule is based on al-Shafi'i's reading of the Quranic verse referring to 'touching women' as a cause for resorting to tayammum. Maliki and Hanafi scholars interpret this verse differently, with the Malikis requiring lustful intent and the Hanafis restricting the meaning to sexual intercourse entirely.

Ghusl, the full ritual bath, is obligatory after sexual intercourse, ejaculation, the end of menstruation (hayd), and postpartum bleeding (nifas). Al-Shafi'i specifies two obligatory acts for a valid ghusl: intention and ensuring water reaches every part of the body's outer surface, including the roots of the hair. Rinsing the mouth and nostrils is obligatory in ghusl for the Shafi'i school, a stricter position than that of the Malikis, who consider these acts recommended.

Tayammum, dry ablution using clean earth, is permitted when water is unavailable or its use would cause harm. Al-Shafi'i holds that tayammum consists of two strikes on the earth: one for the face and one for the hands up to the wrists. It is valid only for the prayer time at hand and must be repeated for each obligatory prayer, since its permissibility is tied to the continuing absence of water. The moment water becomes available, the tayammum is invalidated.

Throughout this chapter, al-Shafi'i grounds each ruling in its Quranic verse or hadith, exemplifying his principle that no ruling is valid without a textual basis.
